content:
Hey friends,

I'm working on writing contributing a Rust implementation for the netconsole module (with support from its maintainer, Breno). As I'm working towards that I have several changes in other subsystems. e.g.

  • string parameters for module! macro
  • early_param/__setup for parameters in module! macro
  • module specified init call
  • console.h wrapper
  • Ip/MAC address structs (parsing/formatting) in rust/kernel/net

and then finally the netconsole module itself, including CONFIG_NETCONSOLE_DYNAMIC and user data support.

I have it working E2E and am wanting to start submitting patch series, especially for the early components.

I'm trying to figure out the best way to approach this. I currently have a series ready to send in for the module changes (7 patches), I think that one is pretty straightforward.

Then I was planning a series with the net lib changes, this also seems straightforward since it's all contained in the rust subsystem.
For the wrappers to netpoll and console, do I also submit those to the rust list but CC the maintainers (e.g. cuda for net poll) or would that go through the net list?
And finally for the netconsole series, I'll talk to Breno, but I imagine that would also go through the net list once the previous changes have gone through rust-next and are available in net's tree?

Essentially, most things should go through the relevant subsystems (i.e. the usual ones you would have normally send it to if it was written in C).

So even if your changes are within rust/, in most cases they would still go through another subsystem. For instance, even the module bits should likely go through "MODULE SUPPORT".

There are exceptions, e.g. sometimes we may act as a fallback, and sometimes different arrangements may be made, but in general, the above is what applies in most cases.

Please take a look at some entries around MAINTAINERS that have rust/ in their F: fields. Also, in case you didn't see it, I have some notes at https://rust-for-linux.com/contributing#the-rust-subsystem.

I hope that helps!

Thank you! I found the contributing guide, which led me here to ask. I appreciate the extra clarification.

My first series is all for the module! macro, so would that be submitted through the rust or linux-module list?
Module maintainers are great to work with, and they prefer you submit via module subsystem. They are picking up rust, but they are still relying on "rust people" to help them review.

For the modules work

  • Please consider the previous work on string params that we cut out of the original submission. Did you base on this or not?
  • Please consider adding support for array and boolean parameters as well, or consider that someone might want to add it in the future.
For the networking side we had a socket patch set a while back. Maybe there is something useful to you in there. I can't remember what parts they did.
There was a student that implemented a network block device driver around those socket abstractions. Sounds like it might be what you need.

My first series is all for the module! macro, so would that be submitted through the rust or linux-module list?

Both lists, please.

To clarify, the patch will get applied through the "MODULE SUPPORT" MAINTAINERS subsystem (this is what I meant with what I said above), but please Cc both set of maintainers and lists (this is because we use rust-for-linux as an index for Rust-related bits, not because it will be applied through the "RUST" entry).

Andreas Hindborg said:

For the modules work

  • Please consider the previous work on string params that we cut out of the original submission. Did you base on this or not?
  • Please consider adding support for array and boolean parameters as well, or consider that someone might want to add it in the future.

Yes, please see https://lore.kernel.org/rust-for-linux/CANiq72k5mU7cACFh8TZC1+NfUQpdrZBToZ-jd75DfCNTfMU1fA@mail.gmail.com/ for the links to the original work by Adam and I.

Sadly, I see there is no record of those links in the Git log, only in the cover letter, and there is no link to the cover letter either, so it is hard to find.

Anyway, this time, let's please make sure to link to the original work and/or give tags if appropriate, e.g. for strings and arrays one of the link above is https://github.com/Rust-for-Linux/linux/pull/110.

Andreas Hindborg said:

For the networking side we had a socket patch set a while back. Maybe there is something useful to you in there. I can't remember what parts they did.

ah, this is a helpful resource, thank you! If the net addresses are going to be in core then that saves me some work.

Miguel Ojeda said:

Andreas Hindborg said:

For the modules work

  • Please consider the previous work on string params that we cut out of the original submission. Did you base on this or not?
  • Please consider adding support for array and boolean parameters as well, or consider that someone might want to add it in the future.

Yes, please see https://lore.kernel.org/rust-for-linux/CANiq72k5mU7cACFh8TZC1+NfUQpdrZBToZ-jd75DfCNTfMU1fA@mail.gmail.com/ for the links to the original work by Adam and I.

Sadly, I see there is no record of those links in the Git log, only in the cover letter, and there is no link to the cover letter either, so it is hard to find.

Anyway, this time, let's please make sure to link to the original work and/or give tags if appropriate, e.g. for strings and arrays one of the link above is https://github.com/Rust-for-Linux/linux/pull/110.

My current changes (preview here) are based on what's in rust-next, using SetOnce that you added. I don't think the string param addition will block subsequent enhancements for array and/or bool. I am a little confused by the GitHub merges though, do those end up in rust-next or will they still need to go through the mailing list?

I am a little confused by the GitHub merges though, do those end up in rust-next or will they still need to go through the mailing list?

PRs in GitHub (including those linked in the lore.kernel.org message I linked above) are (most of them) for work that happened before Rust was merged into the kernel. The reason is that, when Rust support was merged, we were asked to remove most of the code we had, and instead upstream it piece by piece when users appeared. So some code is still out there that was never needed yet. I have some more context at https://rust-for-linux.com/past-branches#rust.

In other words, in the thread I linked above from lore.kernel.org, I was mostly referencing that old work that happened in the past, and that was being upstream by Andreas. So I was giving Andreas the references to the original authors/work so that they could ideally be included in the Git log.

Nowadays all reviews happen in the mailing list, and that issue of "old things in GitHub PRs" almost never happens anymore, since most of the basic bits were already needed one way or the other by the first major users in the upstream kernel.

In summary, you should not submit things via PRs, and the existing PRs are usually very old work. However, if you know about work that overlaps those PRs, please try to reference the old work (both for giving credit to original contributors and as a reference/comparison if you decided on a different approach etc.).
I hope that explains -- you have (un?)luckily hit one of the few remaining cases where this matters... :)

Miguel Ojeda said:

My first series is all for the module! macro, so would that be submitted through the rust or linux-module list?

Both lists, please.

To clarify, the patch will get applied through the "MODULE SUPPORT" MAINTAINERS subsystem (this is what I meant with what I said above), but please Cc both set of maintainers and lists (this is because we use rust-for-linux as an index for Rust-related bits, not because it will be applied through the "RUST" entry).

Sorry, still a bit confused by this. I don't see any MAINTAINERS file in the rust/ directory, so what's the second list I should submit changes to rust/macros and rust/kernel?

Miguel Ojeda said:

Andreas Hindborg said:

For the modules work

  • Please consider the previous work on string params that we cut out of the original submission. Did you base on this or not?
  • Please consider adding support for array and boolean parameters as well, or consider that someone might want to add it in the future.

Yes, please see https://lore.kernel.org/rust-for-linux/CANiq72k5mU7cACFh8TZC1+NfUQpdrZBToZ-jd75DfCNTfMU1fA@mail.gmail.com/ for the links to the original work by Adam and I.

Sadly, I see there is no record of those links in the Git log, only in the cover letter, and there is no link to the cover letter either, so it is hard to find.

Anyway, this time, let's please make sure to link to the original work and/or give tags if appropriate, e.g. for strings and arrays one of the link above is https://github.com/Rust-for-Linux/linux/pull/110.

Thanks for sharing, I didn't realize this work had been started already.

I see that the change you linked was merged, so where is it available now if not in rust-next? Is there more context somewhere on when/why it was removed?

Sorry, still a bit confused by this. I don't see any MAINTAINERS file in the rust/ directory, so what's the second list I should submit changes to rust/macros and rust/kernel?

It is in the top-level directory.
If you haven't seen it yet, then please read https://docs.kernel.org/process/submitting-patches.html (the MAINTAINERS file alongside other very important bits are mentioned there).

I see that the change you linked was merged, so where is it available now if not in rust-next? Is there more context somewhere on when/why it was removed?

It was merged to the development branch (rust, not rust-next) we used when we were out-of-tree. But it wasn't upstreamed to the mainline kernel. Please see https://rust-for-linux.com/past-branches if you want to see a bit more context.
I hope that helps!
Yes, very much so. thanks again! Hoping to having an initial patch out by end of the week. thank you!
You're welcome!

Andreas Hindborg said:

For the modules work

  • Please consider the previous work on string params that we cut out of the original submission. Did you base on this or not?
  • Please consider adding support for array and boolean parameters as well, or consider that someone might want to add it in the future.

I made some changes to my implementation to make it more similar to the prior work (e.g. renamed CStrParam to StringParam), I'll definitely link the #110 PR.

Also, I'll plan on adding array/boolean parameter support in a subsequent series. I don't believe anything in these patches will block that.
Be sure to mention that this work is a dependency for an upcoming Rust implementation of netconsole. Someone is bound to ask.
